author: @deangermeten5629
text: Back-flushing is the problem. The point of desalination is to make pure, potable water. There is no waste-water produced to use for back-flushing. Your Tesla turbine cold vapor boiler is actually a better idea.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: back flushing is a standard technique i believe. But every body would do it different and that's fine with me mate

author: @johnnydeep7089
text:
Unfortunately there is little to no advantage using this system over typical pressurized membrane desalination
You say that you get electricity back but you seem to have missed an important bit of information
You get less energy out than you put in even if you have perfect conductivity plates
This is because the salt recombines less energetically in salty water than fresh
But maybe it can be more efficient than the membrane you say
Nope
Because the membrane system never put in the extra energy of removing the salt from the water to the extent the capacitive system did
And both systems require almost exactly equal pumping pressure and flow rate to generate flow as well as upkeep (the electrodes will get clogged with dust just like the membranes)

author: @peterromo8220
text:
I can't stop thinking about this particular video.  
Have you tried using electrets, or monothermal generators (monothermal from other -- low power/low voltage generators from your other video?)

Your graphene ink is interesting.  I'm going to grab some next month and test it side by side with graphite embedded polyurethane foam.

I'm envisioning a drip feed desalinator.  If electrets or monothermal works for an input power supply (usually considered too low power to be useful), you'd have an excellent portable prototype.

Excellent idea again.

replies.author: @peterromo8220
replies.text:
http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0011916405000603



Would seem to suggest that electret desalination tech is limited by surface area.

replies.text: Peter Romo I do a lot of the videos to inspire others mate in the hope people will pick them up and run with them - i like the ideas you have here - they look promising

author: @luvbotany
text:
If you functionalize your graphene sponge, you can enhance the capacity of each electrode. There are functionalized activated carbons and ion exchange resins like Amberlite with weak and strong bases to use as idea models.

At some point, we are going to be mining the ocean. I can imagine a very large desalinization plant with concentration filters made with functionalized capacitance separating many of the valuables.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree

author: @synespro
text:
Hi Robert / Community: I am attempting a replication. I am using a thick Hydrophilic sponge made by a brand greasemonkey, and it is made from cellulose and "synthetic fibers." I saturated 2 sponges right out of the package , with water resistant conductive ink (my nails are still black lol) , and let the 2sponges dry for 2 days. I then ran cool tap water over the sponge and black graphite water would come out the other side. I thought that some of the ink hadnt dried and the excess liquid was being washed away, but the ink left the sponge to the point where it was turning yellow again. Can anyone help me with what i am doing wrong, or steer me in a better direction.
P.S. I potentially might have solved the electrical connection to the sponges, say they are in a PVC tube enclosure. Drill holes in the sides of the tube and use Gold plated bananna plugs used in home audio systems. I would think the gold plated copper would botth conduct very well and not be comming off in the water. The plugs would just be pressing on the inside wall against the sponge.
Thank you everyone..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: try leaving it longer to dry or oven drying mate - it shouldn't wash out when dry

replies.author: @ADorschner
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith  For reference from wikipedia: "Salt concentration in slightly saline water is around 1,000 to 3,000 ppm" and "Seawater has a salinity of roughly 35,000 ppm, equivalent to 35 grams of salt per one liter (or kilogram) of water."
-so it would seem that the resulting water is certainly drinkable with a single pass.
